---
author: Tristan Muno
date: last-modified
date-format: MMMM D, YYYY
bibliography: ../references.bib
biblio-style: apsr
format:
  revealjs:
    embed-resources: true
    preview-links: false
    slideNumber: true
    incremental: true
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "View source"
    pdfSeparateFragments: false
    footer: QMIR -- {{< meta date >}} -- Week 13
    logo: ./images/uma_ss.png
    theme: theme.scss
editor: source
execute:
  echo: true
  message: false
  warning: false
  freeze: true
  cache: true
---

```{r, message = NA}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
#| eval: true
start_time <- Sys.time()

library(tidyverse) # wrangling and visualization
library(ggpubr) # nice theme

col_1 <- "steelblue"
col_2 <- "#E07B39"
col_3 <- "#7B3F9E"
col_4 <- "goldenrod3"
```

## QMIR 2026 {#sec-title}

::: notes
- Welcome students back for the wrap-up session.
- Today is synthesis and outlook, not new methods. Say it clearly and early.
- Tone: generous, low pressure. They have done the work.
:::

### Week 13: Beyond the Course

![](./images/uma_palace.png){width="100%"} {{< meta author >}} <a href="https://github.com/dertristan" target="_blank" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 10px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-github" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M8 0C3.58 0 0 3.58 0 8c0 3.54 2.29 6.53 5.47 7.59.4.07.55-.17.55-.38 0-.19-.01-.82-.01-1.49-2.01.37-2.53-.49-2.69-.94-.09-.23-.48-.94-.82-1.13-.28-.15-.68-.52-.01-.53.63-.01 1.08.58 1.23.82.72 1.21 1.87.87 2.33.66.07-.52.28-.87.51-1.07-1.78-.2-3.64-.89-3.64-3.95 0-.87.31-1.59.82-2.15-.08-.2-.36-1.02.08-2.12 0 0 .67-.21 2.2.82.64-.18 1.32-.27 2-.27s1.36.09 2 .27c1.53-1.04 2.2-.82 2.2-.82.44 1.1.16 1.92.08 2.12.51.56.82 1.27.82 2.15 0 3.07-1.87 3.75-3.65 3.95.29.25.54.73.54 1.48 0 1.07-.01 1.93-.01 2.2 0 .21.15.46.55.38A8.01 8.01 0 0 0 16 8c0-4.42-3.58-8-8-8"/> </svg> </a> <a href="mailto:tristan.muno@uni-mannheim.de" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 5px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-envelope" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M0 4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h12a2 2 0 0 1 2 2v8a2 2 0 0 1-2 2zm2-1a1 1 0 0 0-1 1v.217l7 4.2 7-4.2V4a1 1 0 0 0-1-1zm13 2.383-4.708 2.825L15 11.105zm-.034 6.876-5.64-3.471L8 9.583l-1.326-.795-5.64 3.47A1 1 0 0 0 2 13h12a1 1 0 0 0 .966-.741M1 11.105l4.708-2.897L1 5.383z"/> </svg> </a> <br> {{< meta date >}}

---

## Today's Agenda {#sec-agenda .smaller}

::: notes
- Preview the four blocks plus the closing slot.
- Equal weight on each block. Even pacing.
- Q&A and exam logistics close the session.
:::

Four blocks of roughly equal weight, plus a buffer for questions and exam logistics:

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.88em"}
| Block | Topic |
|---|---|
| 1 | Recap of the course |
| 2 | Reading classical (frequentist) regression tables |
| 3 | Tooling that makes the code-and-repo grade easy |
| 4 | AI usage policy for the take-home exam |
| Close | Exam logistics, thank-you, questions |
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.82em"}
**No new statistical content today.**
The job of this session is to consolidate what you already have and hand you a clean runway into the exam.
:::

---

## What This Session Is (and Is Not) {#sec-framing .smaller}

::: notes
- Set expectations. Some students will arrive expecting another methods slide deck.
- Say it: today is synthesis, outlook, tooling, and exam logistics.
- Nothing new on the exam. Everything on the exam was already covered.
:::

::: {style="font-size: 0.86em"}
**This session is:**

- A recap that names what you learned and why we taught it this way
- A literacy slide on reading frequentist regression tables
- A tooling pitch that lowers exam anxiety
- A clear policy on AI use during the exam
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.86em"}
**This session is not:**

- A new method or model
- Additional material that you need to study for the exam
- A test of your endurance
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-tip style="font-size: 0.80em"}
You have done the work across twelve weeks.
Today removes friction so you can write a strong take-home exam.
:::

# Block 1: Recap {#sec-block1}

## The Arc of the Course {#sec-arc .smaller}

::: notes
- Walk through the three blocks of the course in plain language.
- Emphasize the cumulative nature: each block built on the previous one.
- Make sure students can articulate this arc to a peer who did not take the course.
:::

The course had three blocks. Each one built on the previous one:

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.84em"}
| Block | Weeks | What you learned |
|---|---|---|
| **I. Scientific programming** | 2 to 5 | R, the tidyverse, `ggplot2`, Quarto, Git. Reproducible workflows. |
| **II. The logic of Bayesian inference** | 6 to 7 | Probability, Bayes' theorem, MCMC, posterior predictive checks. |
| **III. Applied data analysis** | 8 to 12 | Regression, multivariate, interactions, binary outcomes, multilevel models. |
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.80em"}
You can now sit down with a dataset, formulate a research question, fit a Bayesian model, check it, and report it.
That is a real skill.
You did not have it twelve weeks ago.
:::

---

## The Constant Red Line {#sec-constant-line .smaller}

::: notes
- This is the through-line of the entire course. Say it once, point at it, let it sit.
- Same updating logic across every model. Only the likelihood family changes.
- Encouragement message: keep thinking in distributions, not in points.
:::

Across every model you fit, one relationship was constant:

. . .

$$
\text{posterior} \propto \text{likelihood} \times \text{prior}
$$ {#eq-bayes-line}

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.86em"}
This is not a coincidence of notation.
It is the operational core of Bayesian inference.
You start with a prior belief about parameters.
You observe data.
You update your belief in proportion to how well each parameter value explains what you saw.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-tip style="font-size: 0.82em"}
**Keep thinking in distributions.**
A parameter is not a single number to be guessed at.
It is a distribution of plausible values, narrowed by data.
That habit transfers to every quantitative paper you will read.
:::

---

## What Changed Across the Models {#sec-likelihood-table .smaller}

::: notes
- The workflow stays constant. The likelihood is what swaps in and out.
- Walk through the rows: Gaussian for continuous outcomes, Bernoulli for binary, hierarchical prior for grouped data.
- Same brm() call. Same prior arguments. Same MCMC diagnostics. Same PPC. Same LOO.
:::

The same workflow applied to every model you fit.
Only the likelihood family changed:

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
| Week | Likelihood | What changed |
|---|---|---|
| 8 to 10 | Gaussian | Linear predictor, multiple X, interaction terms |
| 11 | Bernoulli | Link function for the probability scale. $\sigma$ drops out. |
| 12 | Gaussian or Bernoulli with a hierarchical prior | Group-level structure. Partial pooling. |
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.84em"}
The `brm()` call, the prior arguments, the convergence diagnostics, the posterior predictive check, and the leave-one-out comparison were the same across the three rows.
Only the `family =` argument and the formula syntax changed.
:::

---

## Why Bayesian First {#sec-why-bayes .smaller}

::: notes
- Pull these seven reasons from the course philosophy. Read them slowly.
- Not a polemic against frequentist statistics. A statement of why this framework is the right starting point.
- Closing line: you are equipped to handle uncertainty. Land that line.
:::

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
We taught Bayesian methods first, deliberately, for seven reasons:

1. **Intuitive updating logic.** Posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior. That is how scientists actually reason when new evidence arrives.
2. **Full uncertainty quantification.** Results are distributions, not point estimates. Credible intervals have a direct probability interpretation.
3. **Unified workflow.** The same `brms` pattern applies from a Gaussian linear model to a multilevel Bernoulli model.
4. **Epistemic humility.** You update beliefs rather than reject or fail to reject. No binary true-or-false dichotomy.
5. **Transparent assumptions.** Priors force you to state your prior beliefs. Researcher degrees of freedom shrink.
6. **Regularization through weakly informative priors.** Implausible parameter values are constrained. Overfitting drops.
7. **Natural fit for hierarchical models.** Partial pooling falls out of the prior structure for free.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-important style="font-size: 0.80em"}
**You are well equipped to handle uncertainty.**
Whatever you do next, you have the conceptual machinery to reason about evidence carefully.
:::

---

## The 8-Step Workflow Is the Durable Takeaway {#sec-workflow-recap .smaller}

::: notes
- This is the operational skill that survives any package or framework change.
- The workflow does not depend on brms. It does not depend on R. It generalises.
- The appendix note: when you write a paper, the substantive story leads. The full workflow goes in a methods appendix. Readers want narrative first.
:::

The eight-step workflow applies to every model you will ever fit:

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.6em"}
| Step | What you do | Why |
|----|------------|--------------|
| 1. DAG + Estimand | Draw the causal graph. State the quantity of interest. | Clarifies what to estimate and what to control for |
| 2. Data Summary | Visualise and describe your variables | Check for patterns, outliers, and model plausibility |
| 3. Formal Model | Write out $L$ and all priors before coding | Forces precision. Makes assumptions explicit. |
| 4. Prior Predictive | Simulate $Y$ from priors. Check plausibility. | Catches implausible priors before MCMC |
| 5. Fit with `brms` | Run `brm()` with explicit priors | Obtains posterior draws |
| 6a. MCMC Diagnostics | Check $\hat{R}$, ESS, trace plots | Confirms convergence before interpreting |
| 6b. PPC | Run `pp_check()` | Checks model adequacy |
| 7. Interpretation | Posterior summaries, predicted probabilities, LOO | Answers the research question |
| 8. Limitations | State what could still bias results | Scientific honesty |
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.76em"}
**In a real write-up, the substantive story lives in the main text.**
The full workflow detail belongs in an appendix.
Readers want the narrative first. They want to know what you found and why it matters.
The methods appendix is for the reader who wants to reproduce or critique your analysis.
:::

# Block 2: Reading Classical Tables {#sec-block2}

```{r}
#| label: setup-block2
#| include: false
#| eval: true
library(knitr) # tables
library(kableExtra) # nicer table styling

bayes_tbl <- tibble(
  Predictor = c("Intercept", "Income (std.)", "Education (std.)", "$\\sigma$"),
  `Posterior median` = c(5.21, 0.34, 0.27, 1.02),
  `95% CrI` = c("[4.78, 5.65]", "[0.18, 0.50]", "[0.10, 0.44]", "[0.96, 1.09]")
)

freq_tbl <- tibble(
  Predictor = c("Intercept", "Income (std.)", "Education (std.)"),
  Estimate = c("5.20***", "0.34***", "0.27**"),
  `(SE)` = c("(0.22)", "(0.08)", "(0.09)"),
  `t-value` = c(23.64, 4.25, 3.00),
  `p-value` = c("<0.001", "<0.001", "0.003")
)
```

## The Same Problem in Both Frameworks {#sec-core-message .smaller}

::: notes
- Frame the block. Bayesian and frequentist statistics try to solve the same problem.
- Estimating the line through a cloud of points is easy.
- Estimating the uncertainty around that line is the hard part. That is where the two frameworks differ.
- MCMC is one of the most sophisticated approaches to the uncertainty problem.
:::

Both frameworks try to do the same thing: separate **noise** from **signal**.

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.86em"}
- Getting a point estimate (the line through the cloud of points) is easy.
- Getting the **uncertainty** around that estimate right is the hard part.
- MCMC, which you used all semester, is among the most sophisticated approaches to that problem.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.82em"}
The choice of framework is mostly a choice of how to represent and report uncertainty.
The substantive story (sign, magnitude, direction) usually survives the translation.
The fine print does not.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-important style="font-size: 0.84em"}
**The frameworks differ fundamentally in one thing: which conditionality they compute.**

$$
\underbrace{P(\theta \mid \text{data})}_{\text{Bayesian}}
\quad \text{vs.} \quad
\underbrace{P(\text{data} \mid \theta)}_{\text{Frequentist}}
$$ {#eq-conditionality}
:::

---

## Side by Side: Same Data, Two Frameworks {#sec-side-by-side .smaller}

::: notes
- Walk through both tables column by column.
- The point estimates are essentially identical. The uncertainty representations differ.
- Reveal the credible interval column first, then the SE-and-stars column.
- The contrast is the lesson. Bayesian: a posterior distribution summarised by quantiles. Frequentist: a sampling-distribution SE plus a test statistic.
:::

A bivariate model of survey support, fit two ways:

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Bayesian (this course)**

```{r}
#| label: tbl-bayes-demo
#| echo: false
bayes_tbl |>
  kable(align = "lrl", escape = FALSE) |>
  kable_styling(font_size = 16, full_width = TRUE)
```

::: {.fragment style="font-size: 0.72em"}
Posterior median plus 95% credible interval.
Probability statement about the parameter.
:::

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Frequentist (typical poli-sci table)**

```{r}
#| label: tbl-freq-demo
#| echo: false
freq_tbl |>
  kable(align = "lrrrr", escape = FALSE) |>
  kable_styling(font_size = 16, full_width = TRUE) |>
  footnote(general = "*** p<0.001, ** p<0.01, * p<0.05", general_title = "")
```

::: {.fragment style="font-size: 0.72em"}
Point estimate, standard error of the sampling distribution, test statistic, and significance stars.
A statement about the procedure, not the parameter.
:::

:::

::::

. . .

::: {.callout-tip style="font-size: 0.78em"}
**Notice:** the point estimates are essentially the same in both tables.
The substantive story (income matters, education matters, both in the expected direction) is identical.
The frameworks differ in how they communicate what we do **not** know.
:::

---

## Coefficient Plots: Same Intuitions {#sec-coef-plots .smaller}

::: notes
- Tables are one display format. Coefficient plots (and marginal-effects plots) are the other common one in modern papers.
- The reading habits are the same as for tables.
- Walk through the heuristics: zero crossing, direction, magnitude, interval width.
- Same Bayesian-vs-frequentist caveat as for tables. The picture looks the same. The interpretation is not.
- Marginal-effects plots are read the same way once the y-axis is the marginal effect rather than the coefficient.
:::

Modern papers do not always present a table.
Many use a **coefficient plot** or a **marginal-effects plot** instead.
The reading habits transfer.

. . .

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {style="font-size: 0.78em"}
- **Does the interval cross zero?** The visual analogue of "excludes zero" in a table.
- **Direction.** Which side of zero the point sits on tells you the sign.
- **Magnitude.** Distance of the point from zero is the effect size.
- **Precision.** Interval width tells you how much the data have pinned the parameter down.
:::

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| label: fig-coef-demo
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 4
#| dpi: 500
#| fig-cap: "Posterior medians with 95% credible intervals."
coef_demo <- bayes_tbl |>
  filter(Predictor %in% c("Income (std.)", "Education (std.)")) |>
  mutate(
    estimate = `Posterior median`,
    lower = c(0.18, 0.10),
    upper = c(0.50, 0.44)
  )

ggplot(coef_demo, aes(x = estimate, y = Predictor)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_pointrange(
    aes(xmin = lower, xmax = upper),
    colour = col_1,
    linewidth = 0.8,
    size = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Coefficient plot of the bivariate survey-support model",
    x = "Coefficient (standardized predictors)",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_pubr()
```

:::

::::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.80em"}
Same picture, different interpretation.
A credible interval on a coefficient plot is a probability statement about the parameter.
A frequentist confidence interval on the same-looking plot is not.
:::

---

## What Translates {#sec-translates .smaller}

::: notes
- These are the intuitions that work in both frameworks. Use them freely.
- Sign and rough magnitude of the coefficient. Always safe.
- Interval width: a wide interval means the data are not pinning the parameter down.
- Model fit and diagnostics: the spirit transfers even when the specific tools differ.
:::

Your Bayesian intuition reads cleanly across to a frequentist table for:

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.86em"}
- **Sign of the coefficient.** Positive means a positive association, negative means a negative association. Same in both frameworks.
- **Rough magnitude of the point estimate.** The Bayesian posterior median and the frequentist point estimate are usually very close.
- **Broadly there vs. tightly null.** A confidence interval that excludes zero by a comfortable margin and a credible interval that excludes zero by a comfortable margin tell roughly the same story.
- **Model fit and diagnostics in spirit.** $R^2$, residual plots, goodness-of-fit checks. The specific tools differ but the question they answer is similar.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.80em"}
For getting the substantive gist of a published paper, your Bayesian reading habits will serve you well.
:::

---

## What Does Not Translate {#sec-does-not-translate .smaller}

::: notes
- These are the four common misreadings that even trained researchers make.
- The 95% confidence interval is not a probability statement about the parameter. Repeat that.
- The standard error is not a posterior SD. Different object, different interpretation.
- The p-value is not "the probability the null is true".
- Significance stars compress everything into a binary signal and throw away information.
:::

Do **not** import the following Bayesian intuitions into a frequentist table:

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
- A **95% confidence interval** is *not* "95% probability the parameter lies in this range". It is a long-run frequency property of the procedure: across many repeated samples, 95% of such intervals would contain the true value.
- A **standard error** is *not* a posterior standard deviation. It is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the estimator.
- A **p-value** is *not* "the probability that the null hypothesis is true".  It is the probability, *if the null were true*, of observing a test statistic at least as extreme as the one computed from your data
- **Significance stars** compress all of this into a binary signal. The information loss is real.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-important style="font-size: 0.78em"}
A confidence interval is a statement about the *procedure*, not the *parameter*.
A credible interval is a statement about the *parameter*, given the data and prior.
Different objects. Do not swap them.
:::

---

## Practical Reading Habits {#sec-practical-reading .smaller}

::: notes
- Be honest. Most readers, including trained researchers, read frequentist tables Bayesianly.
- That is mostly fine for the gist of a paper.
- Be precise when it matters: replication, policy claims, your own thesis.
- This block is about literacy in the broader literature, not advocacy. Stay non-crusading.
:::

::: {style="font-size: 0.86em"}
- Most readers, including trained researchers, casually read frequentist results Bayesianly.
- For getting the gist of a paper, that habit is mostly harmless.
- For **replication claims, policy recommendations, or your own thesis**, be precise about which framework's interval you are reporting and what it actually says.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.82em"}
The course is Bayesian.
This block is **not** advocacy for Bayes over frequentism.
It is literacy: you can read any quantitative paper, and you know which intuitions transfer cleanly and which ones do not.
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.84em"}
The right tool depends on the research question, the data, the audience, and the convention in your subfield.
You now have the conceptual machinery to choose, not just to follow defaults.
:::

---

## Table Packages in R {#sec-table-packages .smaller}

::: notes
- One slide on tooling for regression tables. Quick tour.
- modelsummary is the modern workhorse. stargazer is the old standby for LaTeX. gt is the showpiece option.
- My recommendation for this course: broom.mixed::tidy() piped into kable(). Clean, minimal, works for brms.
:::

When you need to put a regression table in a paper, you have options:

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.86em"}
- **`modelsummary`**: modern, multi-model, supports `brms` directly, sensible defaults.
- **`stargazer`**: long-standing standard for LaTeX output in econ and poli-sci.
- **`gt`**: full table-grammar package, beautiful output, more setup.
:::

. . .

**My recommendation for the take-home exam:**

```r
library(broom.mixed) # tidy() method for brms

broom.mixed::tidy(brms_fit, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 2)
```

. . .

::: {.callout-tip style="font-size: 0.80em"}
Clean, minimal, reproducible.
Pulls posterior summaries straight out of a `brms` fit and renders them in a single line.
:::

# Block 3: Tooling {#sec-block3}

## The Pitch {#sec-tooling-pitch .smaller}

::: notes
- Say this explicitly and openly. Three tools, and the repo grade is a 1.0 by default.
- The substantive analysis is the hard part. Do not also burn time engineering folder structure.
- Frame as anxiety-reducing, not as a flex.
:::

::: {.callout-important style="font-size: 0.92em"}
**If you use these three things together,**
**the code-and-repository portion of your exam grade is at 1.0 level by default,**
**without engineering effort on your part.**
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.88em"}
The three things:

1. **`qqs()`** for project scaffolding
2. **Git** habits already covered in Block I
3. **Air**, the R formatter built into Positron
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.82em"}
The substantive analysis is the hard part of the take-home exam.
Do not also burn time engineering your folder structure.
That is what these tools are for.
:::

---

## QuickQuartoSetup (`qqs`) {#sec-qqs .smaller}

::: notes
- I will run a live demo in the session. These slides are the written reference.
- The package is stable. Default `qqs()` call works out of the box.
- Useful overrides (author, title, statutory declaration, title page) are documented in the README.
- The point is the logic: one command, sane defaults, clean separation of code and writing in one repo.
- Source: github.com/dertristan/qqs. Pull the README at exam time.
:::

A small R package, `qqs`, that bootstraps a Quarto project with one command.

. . .

```r
qqs()
```

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.86em"}
- **One command** to create a fully populated project skeleton
- **Sane defaults**: YAML, bibliography, code template, folder structure all preset
- **One repo, two clear roles**: code lives in `code/`, writing lives at the root
- **No ambiguity**: every file has an obvious place
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
**Source:** [`github.com/dertristan/qqs`](https://github.com/dertristan/qqs)

I will run a quick **live demo** in the session.
These slides are your written reference for after class.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.78em"}
The README documents optional further setup for anyone who wants to explore.
Not exam-relevant. The default `qqs()` call is enough to get started.
:::

---

## The Project Structure {#sec-qqs-structure .smaller}

::: notes
- Walk through the tree. Code in code/. Writing at the root. Data flows raw to processed to final.
- The README documents optional power-user features for interested students.
- This is what graders want to see when they open your repo.
:::

```
project/
  _quarto.yml
  code/
    00_code_template.qmd
  data/
    01_raw/              # never edit
    02_processed/        # intermediate stuff 
    03_final/            # results
  manuscript.qmd
  presentation.qmd
  references.bib
  .gitignore
```

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.84em"}
- **Code lives in `code/`**, writing lives at the root.
- **Data flows** raw to processed to final. The raw folder is never edited.
- **The README documents** optional further setup for advanced students who want to explore. Not required for the exam.
:::

---

## Git Habits {#sec-git-habits .smaller}

::: notes
- Brief recap. Most of this was drilled in Block I.
- Meaningful commit messages: future you and the grader will thank you.
- Push regularly. Local-only work is not safe.
- Never commit credentials. Never commit large data dumps.
- Reinforce: the grader sees your git history, not just the final state.
- Reality check: real data is rarely analysis-ready. Expect to spend time on wrangling.
:::

You already know these. Quick reinforcement:

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.86em"}
- **Meaningful commit messages.** "Add intercept prior justification" beats "update".
- **Push regularly.** A laptop in a backpack is not a backup strategy.
- **Never commit credentials.** API keys, passwords, anything sensitive stays out of the repo.
- **Never commit large data dumps.** Use `.gitignore`. Keep the raw data referenced, not bundled.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-important style="font-size: 0.80em"}
**The grader sees the git history, not just the final repo state.**
A clean, well-paced trail of commits tells me how you worked and what you understood at each step.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.78em"}
**Reality check.**
Do not expect perfectly tidy, analysis-ready data.
Real data needs wrangling, cleaning, and documentation.
That work is part of the exam and shows up in your `code/` folder.
:::

---

## The Air Formatter {#sec-air .smaller}

::: notes
- Air is an R code formatter. Clean, idiomatic style.
- It is built into Positron. No separate install. Same Format Document action you already use for other languages.
- Run it before every commit. Readable code, zero effort, consistent style.
:::

**Air** is an R code formatter that produces clean, idiomatic R style.

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.86em"}
- **Built into Positron.** No separate install. No setup.
- **Invocation:** the standard `Format Document` action in Positron.
- **What it does:** consistent spacing, line breaks, indentation. Idiomatic R style.
- **Cost to you:** one shortcut before each commit.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-tip style="font-size: 0.82em"}
Run Air on every file before you commit.
Your code becomes readable to the grader (and to future-you) with zero conscious effort.
That is a free win on the code-and-repo dimension of your grade.
:::

---

## The Combined Pitch {#sec-combined-pitch .smaller}

::: notes
- Tie it together. Three habits that compound.
- `qqs()` gives you the structure. Git gives you the trail. Air gives you the style.
- The repo dimension of the exam is then a freebie.
:::

These three habits compound:

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.86em"}
- **``qqs()``** gives you the **structure**. Files in the right places. Nothing to invent.
- **Git** gives you the **trail**. Commits show how the analysis evolved.
- **Air** gives you the **style**. Code reads cleanly without conscious effort.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-important style="font-size: 0.86em"}
The repo and code dimensions of your exam grade become a **freebie**.
Your conscious effort goes where it should: the substantive analysis and the written interpretation.
:::

# Block 4: AI Usage Policy {#sec-block4}

## The Headline {#sec-ai-headline .smaller}

::: notes
- Decisive, non-defensive tone. Not "AI is dangerous" and not "AI is magic".
- AI is encouraged for understanding, code, formatting, writing.
- The student takes final responsibility for the submission.
- The only requirement: honesty and transparency.
- AI as tutor framing lowers the threshold for asking basic questions.
:::

::: {.callout-tip style="font-size: 0.92em"}
**AI is encouraged.**
Use it to enhance understanding, improve code, polish formatting, and refine writing.
You take final responsibility for what you submit.
The only thing I require is **honesty and transparency**.
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.86em"}
You will use AI tools during the exam.
That is a realistic prediction, not a guess.
The question is not "whether" but "how to make AI use visible and trustworthy".
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.84em"}
**Think of it as a personal tutor.**
Always available. No question is too basic.
Lean on it when you are stuck.
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.84em"}
The next slide is the one rule that lets both sides trust the result.
:::

---

## The Operational Rule {#sec-ai-rule .smaller}

::: notes
- This is the core of the block. Slow down here.
- Two steps. Your draft first. AI improvement second. Two separate commits.
- The commit message on the second commit names the AI involvement explicitly.
- Examples: "AI spell check", "AI wording improvement", "AI code formatting".
- The git commit message is the audit trail. There is no other AI audit trail in this course.
:::

The **git commit message is the audit trail**. The required workflow:

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.88em"}
1. **Write your own draft** (paragraph, code, analysis). Commit it. Commit message describes **your** work.
2. If you then use AI to improve it (spell check, wording, code style, structure), **commit the improved version separately**. Commit message names the AI involvement explicitly.
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.84em"}
**Example commit messages for the AI step:**

```
AI spell check
AI wording improvement
AI code formatting
AI suggested restructuring of Section 3
```
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-important style="font-size: 0.80em"}
Two commits. One yours. One AI-assisted, labelled as such.
That is the whole policy.
:::

---

## Why This Rule {#sec-ai-why .smaller}

::: notes
- The reasoning matters. Students who understand why will follow the rule even in edge cases.
- It separates your work from AI-assisted work in the git history.
- The grader can see the trajectory and judge accordingly.
- Without the convention, AI use is invisible.
- Scientific standards rest on voluntary integrity. This is its operational form for AI.
:::

::: {style="font-size: 0.86em"}
- It **separates your work from AI-assisted work** in the git history. Two distinct commits, two distinct authors of the improvement.
- The grader can **see the trajectory** and judge accordingly. The before-and-after is visible.
- Without this convention, **AI use is invisible**. The grader has to guess. The student has to self-report after the fact. Neither is robust.
- Scientific standards rest on **voluntary integrity**. This is the operational form that integrity takes for AI in this course.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.80em"}
The rule is generous on what AI can be used for.
It is strict on one thing only: be honest about when you used it.
:::

---

## Acceptable Use {#sec-ai-ok .smaller}

::: notes
- Concrete examples. These are not edge cases. They are the normal modes of AI use in this course.
- Asking AI to explain a concept when stuck.
- Pasting a draft into AI for proofreading.
- Asking AI to clean up your code.
- Asking AI to help debug an error message.
- Calibrate trust by domain: stronger on formal logic and code, weaker on narrow IR substance.
:::

::: {style="font-size: 0.88em"}
**All of these are acceptable, encouraged, and easy to attribute:**

- Asking AI to **explain a concept** when you are stuck.
- Pasting your **draft into AI for proofreading**.
- Asking AI to **clean up code you wrote**.
- Asking AI for **help debugging an error message**.
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.84em"}
**Calibrate trust by domain.**
AI is more reliable on formal logic, programming, and general well-covered topics.
On narrow substantive IR questions it is less reliable.
Treat narrow factual claims with scepticism and check the source.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.82em"}
In each case: do the work yourself first, commit it, then commit the AI-assisted improvement with a transparent commit message.
:::

---

## Unacceptable Use {#sec-ai-not-ok .smaller}

::: notes
- Two clear violations. Both are about hiding AI involvement.
- Generating an analysis from scratch via AI and submitting it as your own.
- Editing AI output before committing so that the AI step is hidden.
- The pattern is the same: the git history misrepresents who did the work.
:::

::: {style="font-size: 0.86em"}
**These are not acceptable, and the git history will reveal them:**

- **Generating an analysis from scratch via AI** and submitting it as your own work, with no acknowledgement.
- **Hiding AI involvement** by editing AI output before committing, without naming the AI step in a commit message.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-important style="font-size: 0.80em"}
The common thread: the git history misrepresents who did the work.
That is the line.
Cross it and the work is no longer yours.
:::

---

## Using AI Well {#sec-ai-effective .smaller}

::: notes
- Policy is settled. This slide is about doing it well.
- Two habits drive most of the difference between useful and useless output.
- Context management: state what you want and what you do not want.
- Window hygiene: short, focused chats. Start fresh when the task changes.
- These habits apply to coding assistants, writing assistants, and explanation prompts equally.
:::

Policy aside, two habits drive most of the difference between useful and useless AI output.

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.86em"}
- **Context management is the core skill.** State what you want and what you do **not** want. The vaguer the prompt, the more the model fills the gaps by imputing things you never specified. That is where hallucination comes from.
- **A fuller context window degrades performance.** Be deliberate about what each prompt actually needs. Avoid bloated, long-running chats where noise accumulates across many turns. Start a fresh chat when the task changes.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-tip style="font-size: 0.80em"}
Clear ask. Clear non-ask. Fresh chat per task.
Three habits, one outcome.
:::

---

## Grading Scheme {#sec-grading-scheme .smaller}

::: notes
- Be open: the detailed grading scheme is distributed with the exam, not yet.
- Four announced dimensions. State them clearly.
- Two of the four (formalities and code-and-repo) are largely covered by `qqs()` and Air.
- That is the connection back to Block 3.
:::

The **detailed grading scheme will be distributed together with the exam itself.**

. . .

The **four announced dimensions** are:

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
1. **Argumentation.** What you are doing and why. Assessed from the written PDF.
2. **Interpretation.** Are results interpreted correctly, with limitations and blind spots acknowledged. Assessed from the written PDF.
3. **Formalities.** Does the PDF look like a typical research article. **`qqs()` is half the deal here.**
4. **Code and repo.** Can a human trace what you did, where, and why. Informative commit messages. AI usage transparently noted in commit history. **`qqs()` and the Air formatter are half the deal here.**
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-tip style="font-size: 0.80em"}
Two of the four dimensions are mostly solved by good tooling and good habits.
That is on purpose.
Your conscious effort belongs in dimensions 1 and 2: argumentation and interpretation.
:::

# Closing {#sec-closing}

## Exam Logistics {#sec-exam-logistics .smaller}

::: notes
- Exam window dates: insert the placeholder. Do not invent dates.
- Distribution via GitHub Classroom, like every weekly homework.
- The repo at the deadline is what gets graded. There is no separate submission upload.
- Deliverables: reproducible repo, rendered PDF (data/methods/analysis sections, about 10 pages of main text), statutory declaration.
- No chat histories. That is intentional. The commit-message convention replaces them.
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.84em"}
**Distribution and submission:**

- Distributed via **GitHub Classroom**, the same way as the weekly homework.
- The **version of the repo at the deadline is what gets graded.** There is no separate upload.
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
**What to submit (inside the repo):**

- A **reproducible repository** that renders end to end.
- A **rendered PDF** structured like the data, methods, and analysis sections of a research article. Roughly **10 pages of main text**.
- A **signed statutory declaration** of authorship.

**No chat histories.** The git commit-message convention is the AI audit trail.
:::

---

## Thank You {#sec-thanks .smaller}

::: notes
- One warm sentence. Mean it.
- Make sure students know I am available for questions during the exam window.
- Open the floor for questions after this slide.
:::

::: {style="font-size: 0.92em"}
Thank you for showing up, for asking good questions, for the late-night Git struggles, and for taking the leap into Bayesian inference.
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.86em"}
Good luck on the exam.
You are ready.
:::

---

## {#sec-final}

::: notes
- Final slide. Pause. Smile. End the course on a warm note.
- "Keep your priors wide" doubles as a methods joke and a life motto.
:::

::: {style="text-align: center; font-size: 2.4em; margin-top: 2em;"}
**Keep your priors wide!**
:::

```{r}
#| label: session-info
#| eval: true
#| include: false
sessionInfo()
```

```{r}
#| label: exec-time
#| eval: true
#| include: false
Sys.time() - start_time
```
